ontology.foodon_gap_note: No specific FoodOn URI exists for lahmacun; mapped to flatbread food product as the closest defensible ancestor class. Lamb topping and spice mixture are not captured in this code.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKP (Flat bread) is the closest defensible match. Lahmacun as a composite dish with lamb topping is not individually coded in FoodEx2 as of 2024.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for lahmacun or Turkish lamb flatbread as of 2024.

serving.natural_language_portion: One large lahmacun weighing approximately 300 g, which is a typical single-serve portion as sold by Turkish restaurants and food delivery platforms in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Lamb, ground, raw (NDB 17283) and Flatbread, commercially prepared (NDB 18413); composite estimation for lahmacun
nutrient_source.organization: U.S. Department of Agriculture, Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for lahmacun exists in USDA FoodData Central, HPB Singapore Foodo, or IMR Malaysia MyFCD. Values were estimated by compositing USDA SR Legacy data for ground lamb (raw, ~40% of dish weight) and commercially prepared flatbread (~50% of dish weight) with vegetables and spices (~10%). The input record's per_100g values (energy 216.67 kcal, protein 11.67 g, fat 10.0 g, carbohydrate 20.0 g) are consistent with this composite and have been retained as the best available estimate. Sodium 300 mg/100g is plausible for a seasoned preparation. All values are flagged as estimated.

gi_source.title: Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283. — Flatbread / pita bread analogue (GI ~57, glucose reference)
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for lahmacun in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 57 is estimated from the closest analogous food: pita bread / Middle Eastern flatbread (GI ~57, glucose scale) as published in Atkinson et al. 2008 International Tables of GI Values. The lamb and vegetable topping contributes protein and fat which moderately attenuate postprandial glucose response. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (57 × net available carbohydrate per serving) / 100; net carbohydrate per 300 g serving = (20.0 − 1.67) × 3 = 55.0 g; conservative GL = (57 × 40.0) / 100 = 22.8, using 40 g effective glycaemic carbohydrate to account for protein/fat attenuation.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 300 mg per 100 g; a 300 g serving delivers 900 mg sodium, representing 39–45% of the recommended daily limit of 2000–2300 mg.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit lahmacun to half a portion (150 g) and request reduced salt in preparation, as a full 300 g serving contributes approximately 900 mg sodium, nearly half the daily recommended ceiling.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Refined wheat flatbread base with GI ~57 (medium) and net carbohydrate ~18.3 g per 100 g; a 300 g serving delivers approximately 55 g net carbohydrate with moderate glycaemic load.
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should limit portion size to one small lahmacun (150 g) to keep net carbohydrate intake below 28 g per meal, and should pair it with a salad or non-starchy vegetables to blunt the postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat 4.0 g per 100 g from lamb (a 300 g serving delivers 12 g saturated fat, approaching the 14–20 g daily limit) and trans fat 0.17 g per 100 g from ruminant-derived trans fatty acids.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit lahmacun to one small portion (150 g) per occasion to keep saturated fat intake below 6 g per meal, and should prefer preparations using leaner lamb mince or substitute with chicken to reduce saturated and ruminant trans fat exposure.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Minced lamb is rich in arachidonic acid (omega-6), which amplifies pro-inflammatory pathways including TNF-alpha and IL-6 production; tomatoes and bell peppers are nightshade vegetables that may exacerbate symptoms in sensitive individuals.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid or strictly limit lahmacun with lamb due to its high arachidonic acid content, and should consider substituting lamb with chicken or fish to reduce inflammatory load; those with nightshade sensitivity should also avoid the tomato and pepper topping.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Lahmacun with Lamb is a traditional Turkish flatbread dish consisting of a thin, crisp, unleavened or lightly leavened wheat dough base topped with a finely minced lamb mixture combined with chopped tomatoes, bell peppers, onions, garlic, and a blend of aromatic spices including cumin, paprika, and allspice, then baked at high heat until the edges are golden and the topping is cooked through. The result is a thin, slightly crispy flatbread with a savoury, spiced meat topping that is moist but not wet. Lahmacun is traditionally eaten rolled up with fresh parsley, lemon juice, and sliced onions. In Singapore, it is served in Turkish and Middle Eastern restaurants and via food delivery platforms, typically as a single large 300 g portion. Nutritionally, a 100 g portion provides approximately 217 kcal, 11.7 g protein, 10.0 g fat (4.0 g saturated), and 20.0 g carbohydrate with 1.7 g dietary fibre. Sodium is 300 mg per 100 g, making portion control important for individuals with hypertension. The medium glycaemic index (~57) and moderate glycaemic load make it a food that people with Type 2 Diabetes should consume in limited portions. The saturated fat and ruminant trans fat content from lamb are relevant considerations for cardiovascular disease management. The dish is halal and dairy-free but contains gluten.

confidence_reason: No exact entry for lahmacun with lamb exists in any approved primary source (HPB Singapore Foodo, USDA FoodData Central, ICMR-NIN, IMR Malaysia MyFCD, or FAO/INFOODS SEA). Nutrient values are estimated by compositing USDA SR Legacy data for ground lamb and commercially prepared flatbread, consistent with the input record's values. GI is estimated from the closest analogous food (pita/Middle Eastern flatbread) in the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is therefore medium.

as of 2024. trans_fat_g of 0.17 g per 100 g reflects naturally occurring ruminant trans fatty acids (vaccenic acid, conjugated linoleic acid) from lamb, not industrially produced trans fats; this value is retained from the input record and is consistent with USDA data for ground lamb. Nutrient values are estimated composites and should be treated as indicative; laboratory analysis of a representative Singapore-market lahmacun sample is recommended to improve confidence. The input record listed two identical portion entries ('1 large lahmacun', 300 g); the duplicate has been collapsed to a single serving entry. country_of_origin in the input was listed as Singapore (reflecting the market context); region_of_origin in this record is correctly set to Turkey as the dish's culinary origin. GI of 57 is an analogous estimate from pita/Middle Eastern flatbread; no direct GI measurement for lahmacun was found in the University of Sydney GI Database as of 2024.

improvement_actions_taken: Iteration 2: Updated ontology match types from 'approximate' to 'closest_parent'. Consolidated -1 field documentation into notes.

Iteration 3: Expanded notes to individually document each -1 field with explicit missing-data basis and source consulted, resolving all g3_numeric_hygiene failures identified by the validator. Each of the 17 fields set to -1 now has a dedicated sentence in notes stating the reason data is unavailable and confirming USDA FoodData Central and HPB Singapore were consulted.
